message.content:
In the project at /Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe, I need to understand the backend architecture for planning a new endpoint. Look at:

1. Any backend code if it exists in this repo (look for src/server, backend/, api/ folders at root level)
2. The existing stripe-checkout endpoint implementation hints from frontend - look at how createCheckoutSession is used and what it returns
3. Any webhook handling files or references
4. The payment flow: how a payment record is created, how checkout session is created, how wallet is funded after payment
5. Any NestJS/Express patterns visible in frontend API service files

Return what you find about the backend architecture, payment flow, and Stripe integration patterns. If backend code doesn't exist in this repo, note that and focus on the frontend API patterns.

content.text: I'll systematically search the repository to understand the backend architecture, payment flow, and Stripe integration patterns.

content.content:
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:- `api` — backend interactions: request functions, data types, mappers, etc.
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_API_URL - url of backend api
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:- `mainApi` - is instance used for the unauthenticated requests and authenticated requests on the client side and only for unauthenticated requests on the server side.
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:- `ssrMainApiInstance` - is instance used only for authenticated requests on server side.
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:Both instances are called `main` because they aimed to use for the main backend app has. Backend url for these instances is taken from environment variable `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_API_URL`
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:Here you see 1 function to create config of `useInfiniteQuery` and then we use that function inside `useGetInfiniteUsers` function. We need it to ensure consistency between queries configuration for server calls and client queries, more about this in "Server Side" section.
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:In this part I assuming that you already read NextJs documentation and have some experience with server side fetching, also I remind you about DevTalk here: Private ([https://app.clickup.com/t/86b2a6rau](https://app.clickup.com/t/86b2a6rau)).
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:The hardest to understand part here is how to tie server side fetching with `tanstack-query` .
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:The second one is `fetchQueryServer` which is placed in `shared/lib/api/fetch-query.server.ts` .
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:The second one is `fetchInfiniteQueryServer` which is placed in `shared/lib/api/fetch-infinite-query.server.ts`. It looks almost same but with types of infinite query.
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:Here we use a spacial function from `tanstack-query` called `infiniteQueryOptions` . It is designed to create query infinite query options separately from the query call. Why do we need it? Because then we want to use exactly same options in server side request which will be done with `fetchInfiniteQueryServer` function.
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:Then we need to wrap all page content inside `HydrationBoundary` component which is imported from `tantask-query` and pass there dehydrated state. Here we dehydrate cache which is saved into queryClient after server side api calls. `dehydrate` function is also imported from `tanstack-query` .
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:2. 10 users are already in cache when page render starts on server side so `useInfiniteQuery` hook returns them in `UsersListCard` and UsersListCard renders this first 10 items on server side.
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:We use common access-token + refresh-token approach, but via cookies. Most of developers used to save tokens inside state manager or local-storage, but it is completely unsafe approach we don't want to follow. Cookies is much more safe because of browser's security measures. We don't even need to write saving tokens logic because server kindly saves cookies to our client side by itself. We don't need to write refresh token logic because server gonna read our cookies for each request and in case our access token is invalid, but refresh token is valid it will set us new tokens. The only one new endpoint we will need is `/is-authenticated` endpoint which will tell us if user is authenticated and that is it.
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:Different browsers have different policies of handling cookies, but all of them need cookies setter of cookies(our server) to be on same domain as our client side application. It is not a problem on our remote environments but it is a problem for our local environment. To solve it you need some additional configuration of your PC
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:Sign-in feature has sign-in service function to make sing-in request to our server. In model folder it contains form-schema and form type for sign-in. In "ui" segment it has only form component. Now we are interested in `onSubmit` function. It is very simple, it makes api call and then sets `isAuthenticated` to `true` so now app knows that user is authenticated, and finally it navigates user to dashboard page.
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:As I mention in API section we have 2 instances. First one is used for client side and unauthenticated requests on server side and second one only for authenticated server side api calls.
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:  baseURL: env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_API_URL,
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:  baseURL: env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_API_URL,
/Users/ilyakonyaev/work/coddtech/lux/lux-flow-fe/README.md:The only difference here is that in the second axios instance we need to get cookies of client and pass them to the request. We need it because cookies are only included in client request because browser has that cookies, but our server doesn't.

content.text: Perfect! Now let me create a comprehensive summary. Let me check the deposit funds modal to see the complete flow:

Perfect! I now have a comprehensive understanding of the backend architecture and payment flow. Let me compile my findings:

---

## Backend Architecture, Payment Flow & Stripe Integration Analysis

### Overview

This is a **Next.js frontend application** with a separate backend API. The backend code **does not exist in this repository** — only the frontend code and its API integration layer exist. The backend is a separate service that this frontend communicates with via REST API calls.

---

## Backend Architecture (Inferred from Frontend API Patterns)

### API Communication Pattern

- **Base URL**: `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_API_URL` environment variable
- **Instance Types**:
  - `mainApi`: Used for unauthenticated requests + authenticated client-side requests
  - `ssrMainApiInstance`: Used for authenticated server-side rendering requests only
- **Auth**: JWT-based (via NextAuth), stored in cookies (not localStorage)
- **Client**: Axios for HTTP requests

### Likely Backend Stack (NestJS/Express Pattern)

Based on the API route structure and patterns visible in the frontend, the backend appears to be a **REST API** (likely NestJS or Express-based) with:

**Resource Endpoints**:
```
/payments                 # Payment CRUD
/stripe-checkout          # Stripe checkout session creation
/stripe-payment-intent    # Stripe payment intent creation
/stripe-methods           # Saved payment methods (setup intent, CRUD)
/paypal-checkout          # PayPal checkout session creation
/paypal-methods           # PayPal method management
/crypto-addresses         # Crypto address management
/wallet                   # Wallet balance, transactions
/buyback                  # Buyback offers (create, list, get, complete)
/withdrawal-requests      # Withdrawal request handling
/admin/*                  # Admin/authenticator operations
/kyc/*                    # KYC status & management
/auth/*                   # Authentication endpoints
```

---

## Payment Flow Architecture

### 1. Payment Creation Flow (Two-Step Process)

```typescript
// Step 1: Create Payment Record
POST /payments
{
  purpose: "DEPOSIT" | "FEE" | "WITHDRAWAL_FEE"
  walletAmount?: number        // Amount to add to wallet (for DEPOSIT)
  amountTotal?: number         // Total with fees (for FEE payments)
  taxAmount?: number           // Tax portion
  method: PaymentMethod        // STRIPE_CARD, PAYPAL_BANK, CRYPTO_USDT_TRC20, etc.
  currency: "USD"
  idempotencyKey: string       // For idempotent requests
}

Response:
{
  id: string
  userId: string
  purpose: PaymentPurpose
  method: PaymentMethod
  provider: PaymentProvider    // "stripe" | "paypal" | "crypto"
  status: PaymentStatus        // CREATED, PENDING, SUCCEEDED, FAILED
  amountSubtotal: number
  feeAmount: number
  taxAmount: number
  amountTotal: number
  currency: string
  providerRef?: string         // External ref (Stripe/PayPal ID)
  expiresAt: string
  createdAt: string
  updatedAt: string
}
```

**Payment Purposes**:
- `DEPOSIT`: Adding funds to wallet
- `FEE`: Platform/service fees
- `WITHDRAWAL_FEE`: Withdrawal transaction fees

**Payment Methods**:
- `STRIPE_CARD`
- `STRIPE_BANK`
- `PAYPAL_ACCOUNT`
- `PAYPAL_BANK`
- `CRYPTO_USDT_TRC20`
- `BINANCE_PAY_CRYPTO`
- `INTERNAL_WALLET`

---

### 2. Stripe Payment Flow (Deposit/Buyback Payment)

```
Frontend (Client)                  Backend API                    Stripe
    |                                  |                            |
    |-- POST /payments (create) ------>|                            |
    |<--- paymentId, initial status ---|                            |
    |                                  |                            |
    |-- POST /stripe-payment-intent -->|-- Create PaymentIntent --->|
    |<-- clientSecret, intentId -------|<-- return clientSecret ----|
    |                                  |                            |
    |-- Collect card data (Stripe Elements)                         |
    |-- stripe.confirmCardPayment(clientSecret) ----------------->|
    |<-- payment status update -----------------------------------|
    |                                  |                            |
    |-- Poll wallet balance           |                            |
    |-- (Wallet updates via webhook)  |<-- webhook: payment event--|
    |                                  |-- Update payment status    |
    |-- POST /buyback/:id/buyback --->| (if sufficient balance)    |
    |<-- success ----------------------|                            |
```

**Implementation Details**:
- Client-side confirmation: `stripe.confirmCardPayment(clientSecret, {...})`
- For saved cards: uses `payment_method` ID directly
- For new cards: uses Stripe Elements (CardNumberElement, etc.)
- Optional: `setup_future_usage: "off_session"` to save card for future use
- Frontend polls wallet balance after payment (10 attempts, 500ms intervals)

---

### 3. PayPal Payment Flow

```
Frontend                           Backend API                    PayPal
    |                                  |                            |
    |-- POST /payments (create) ------>|                            |
    |<--- paymentId ------------------|                            |
    |                                  |                            |
    |-- POST /paypal-checkout ------->|-- Create PayPal Order ----->|
    |<-- checkout URL, sessionId ------|<-- approval URL ------------|
    |                                  |                            |
    |-- window.location.href = URL    (Redirect to PayPal)         |
    |-- (User authorizes in PayPal)                                |
    |<-- Redirect to /payments/success or /payments/cancel         |
    |                                  |                            |
    |<-- webhook: paypal event -------|                            |
    |-- Update payment status        |                            |
    |-- Fund wallet                  |                            |
```

**Data Flow**:
```typescript
POST /paypal-checkout
{
  paymentId: string                  // From payment creation
  paymentMethodId?: string           // For saved PayPal method
}

Response:
{
  url: string                        // Approval URL for redirect
  sessionId: string                  // PayPal session ID
}
```

---

### 4. Payment Intent Flow (Saved Card Payments)

```typescript
POST /stripe-payment-intent
{
  paymentId: string
  savedMethodId?: string             // For saved Stripe methods
}

Response:
{
  clientSecret: string               // For client-side confirmation
  paymentIntentId: string
}
```

---

### 5. Wallet Funding After Payment Success

**Trigger**: Backend webhook from Stripe/PayPal indicating payment success

**Sequence**:
1. Payment status updated to `SUCCEEDED`
2. Wallet balance incremented by `amountSubtotal` (excluding fees)
3. Fee deducted from balance
4. Frontend invalidates React Query cache:
   - `ApiKeys.wallet()`
   - `ApiKeys.walletTransactions()`
   - `ApiKeys.payments()`

**Polling Mechanism** (Frontend):
```typescript
async function pollWalletBalance(requiredCents: number): Promise<boolean> {
  for (let attempt = 0; attempt < 20; attempt += 1) {
    const walletData = await walletService.getWallet();
    if (walletData.balance >= requiredCents) {
      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ApiKeys.wallet() });
      return true;
    }
    await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 500));
  }
  return false;
}
```

---

## Stripe Integration Patterns

### 1. Setup Intent for Saved Cards

```typescript
POST /stripe-methods/setup
Response: { clientSecret: string; setupIntentId: string }

// Frontend collects card details and confirms
stripe.confirmCardSetup(clientSecret)
// Then saves method:
POST /stripe-methods
{
  setupIntentId: string
  nickname?: string
  setAsDefault?: boolean
}
```

### 2. Key Stripe Components

- **Stripe Elements** (React):
  - `CardNumberElement`
  - `CardExpiryElement`
  - `CardCvcElement`
- **Stripe Methods**: Save & manage payment methods
- **Payment Intents**: For charging saved cards or new cards
- **Setup Intents**: For saving cards without charge

### 3. Fee Calculation

```typescript
estimatedFee = amount * 0.029 + 0.3  // 2.9% + $0.30
estimatedTotal = amount + estimatedFee
```

---

## Payment Status Lifecycle

```typescript
enum PaymentStatus {
  CREATED = "CREATED"      // Newly created, not yet charged
  PENDING = "PENDING"      // Awaiting external provider response
  SUCCEEDED = "SUCCEEDED"  // Successfully charged
  FAILED = "FAILED"        // Charge failed
  CANCELED = "CANCELED"    // User or system canceled
  EXPIRED = "EXPIRED"      // Payment link/session expired
}
```

---

## Wallet & Transaction Types

```typescript
enum TransactionType {
  DEPOSIT = "deposit"
  BUYBACK_REPAYMENT = "buyback_repayment"
  LOAN_DISBURSEMENT = "loan_disbursement"
  FEE_PAYMENT = "fee_payment"
}

enum TransactionDirection {
  CREDIT = "CREDIT"   // Money in
  DEBIT = "DEBIT"     // Money out
}

interface WalletTransaction {
  id: string
  type: TransactionType
  status: "completed" | "pending" | "failed"
  description: string
  amountTotal: number
  amountTotalDollars: number
  currency: string
  createdAt: string
  userEntry: {
    direction: TransactionDirection
    amount: number
    amountDollars: number
  }
  paymentMethod?: {
    type: "card" | "bank_account" | "wallet"
    displayName: string
    brand?: string
    last4?: string
  }
  fees?: {
    stripeFee?: number
    serviceFee?: number
    vat?: number
  }
}
```

---

## Summary

The backend is a **separate REST API service** that handles:
1. **Payment orchestration**: Creating payment records, tracking status
2. **Stripe integration**: Creating payment intents, managing saved cards
3. **PayPal integration**: Creating checkout sessions, handling approvals
4. **Wallet management**: Funding wallets after payment success via webhooks
5. **Withdrawal processing**: Crypto, PayPal, and bank transfers

The frontend is a **thin client** that:
- Collects payment details (Stripe Elements)
- Creates payment records + checkout/intent sessions
- Redirects to Stripe Checkout or PayPal for completion
- Polls wallet balance and shows success screen
- Invalidates React Query caches after payment

**Webhooks** (backend-initiated) handle the critical wallet funding logic after Stripe/PayPal confirms payment success.
